abstract: Mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH) are among the most efficient catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline media, yet the atomistic origin of their high activity—and whether Ni or Fe serves as the active center—remains a subject of debate. In this work, we use operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD-XAS) combined with density functional theory (DFT+U) to investigate the local structure and electronic states of (Ni,Fe)OOH across a wide composition range. Our results show that at iron concentrations below ~25%, Fe³⁺ ions substitute for Ni³⁺/⁴⁺ in the γ-NiOOH lattice. In this environment, the Fe–O bond lengths are significantly compressed (~1.90 Å) compared to pure iron oxyhydroxide, a distortion imposed by the edge-sharing [NiO₆] octahedral framework. As the iron content increases further, a phase-separated, less active γ-FeOOH component emerges. Theoretical calculations demonstrate that these substituted Fe sites possess near-optimal adsorption energies for OER intermediates, explaining the observed 500-fold enhancement in catalytic activity compared to pure Ni and Fe parent phases. Conversely, Ni sites remain substantially less active. These findings identify Fe as the true active site in (Ni,Fe)OOH and highlight how the host lattice can tune a dopant's local environment to reach peak electrochemical performance.
